data.title: Layer stratification does not detect file-level overlap between same-layer stories causing mass merge conflicts
data.description:
## Observed
S1 (075a-b781) and S2 (af9e-77fc) were both placed in Layer 0 (parallel execution) by preplanning. Both stories modified the same ~80 .sh files — S1 on comment lines, S2 on code lines. Running them in parallel worktrees produced 73 merge conflicts when the orchestrator merged S2's branch after S1's.

## Impact
73 merge conflicts required manual conflict resolution, adding ~30 minutes to the sprint. The conflict analyzer correctly identified them all as TRIVIAL (different lines in same files), but the volume made it fragile. Could have been avoided entirely by ordering S1 → Layer 0, S2 → Layer 1.

## Root Cause
The layer stratification algorithm uses ticket-level dependency links to determine layers. It does not analyze file-level overlap (which files each story will modify) to detect potential merge conflicts between parallel stories.

## Expected
When two stories in the same layer are predicted to modify overlapping files (by inspecting their task `## Files to Modify` sections or file impact analysis), the stratification should place them in separate layers or flag the overlap for the orchestrator.

## Fix Direction
In the preplanning or implementation-plan layer-stratify step, after tasks are created, run a cross-story file impact comparison. If two same-layer stories share any file in their `## Files to Modify` tables, assign the later one to the next layer. The sprint drift-check already has file-overlap detection (`sprint-drift-check.sh`) — the same logic could seed the layer assignment.
